medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia is a reasonable option for the mild lesion tenderness and is safe with apixaban. Preferred over NSAIDs specifically because it does not increase bleeding risk. No dose is specified in the source note, so none is asserted here - standard OTC adult dosing per label.

An NSAID (or aspirin) must NOT be recommended for the pain in this patient - concurrent apixaban means additive bleeding risk and NSAIDs would worsen bleeding from the lesion and raise gastrointestinal bleeding risk. Recommending one is an error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: concurrent apixaban anticoagulation - additive bleeding risk
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: apixaban + NSAID/aspirin - additive bleeding risk, clinically relevant because this patient is anticoagulated

medications.indication_rationale: Field-directed therapy (topical fluorouracil or imiquimod) is legitimate management of an actinic keratosis field, but it is a clinician-initiated treatment that follows in-person assessment - it must NOT be started via telehealth on an acutely inflamed, bleeding lesion before invasive squamous cell carcinoma has been examined for and excluded. Deferred to the GP/skin-cancer review.
contraindications_in_this_case: acutely inflamed, broken and bleeding lesion - not the moment to start an irritant field agent

medications.indication_rationale: There is no purulence, spreading erythema, fever or systemic upset - there is no clinical infection. Empiric antibiotics are not indicated and prescribing them is an error of commission (antimicrobial stewardship).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Leave the protective dressing on the lesion for about 48 hours, and do not pick, scratch or rub off the scale or crust.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Allows the minor trauma to clot and heal and prevents repeat bleeding episodes - especially important because apixaban makes the lesion bleed more readily.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear a wide-brimmed hat and apply SPF50+ sunscreen to the scalp, face and backs of the hands whenever outdoors; carry out regular self-checks of the sun-damaged areas and note or photograph any spot that grows, thickens, ulcerates or bleeds.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces further ultraviolet field damage and the risk of progression to squamous cell carcinoma, and helps detect a changing lesion early.

patient_education_points.point: The bleeding came from minor trauma to a fragile, sun-damaged spot, and your blood thinner makes such spots bleed a little more easily. Firm pressure and a simple dressing let it settle and heal.

Because this particular spot has recently changed and bled, it needs to be looked at in person - a video call cannot rule out a skin cancer, and your GP or a skin clinic may examine it closely and take a small sample (biopsy) if needed.
If it bleeds again, press firmly and continuously on it for a full 15 minutes without lifting off to peek, then apply a clean dressing.
Do not use ibuprofen, aspirin or other anti-inflammatory painkillers for the soreness - they can increase bleeding on top of your blood thinner. Paracetamol is a safer choice.
Protecting your scalp with a hat and sunscreen from now on helps stop further sun damage and new spots.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt in-person GP or skin-cancer clinic review within a few days, and no later than 1-2 weeks, for examination of the changed lesion
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: The symptomatic left parietal lesion (dermoscopy and consideration of cryotherapy or biopsy to exclude SCC), the broader field of sun damage for field-directed therapy, healing of the bled area, and a review of anticoagulation/bleeding.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Bleeding that will not stop after 15 minutes of firm continuous pressure, a rapidly enlarging or hard painful lump, an ulcer that will not heal, or spreading redness, warmth, pus or fever - seek same-day or emergency care as below.

must_include_items: Recognise this is a recently CHANGED, bleeding lesion on heavily sun-damaged skin that cannot be characterised or excluded as benign via telehealth
Arrange prompt in-person GP / skin-cancer review for dermoscopy and consideration of biopsy (within days to 1-2 weeks)
Give correct patient-deliverable haemostasis advice: firm continuous direct pressure for 15 minutes plus a simple non-adherent dressing
Identify anticoagulation (apixaban) as the reason for easy bleeding and avoid NSAIDs/aspirin for analgesia
Safety-net for uncontrolled bleeding and for signs of secondary infection
acceptable_alternatives: Routine GP skin check within 1-2 weeks is acceptable in place of 'within a few days' PROVIDED a clear malignancy safety-net is given (matches the source note's 7-14 day disposition)
Paracetamol (or no analgesia) is acceptable for the mild pain in place of any specific analgesic recommendation
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person examination/biopsy of the changed lesion
Reassuring the patient the lesion is benign without any referral
Not giving direct-pressure haemostasis / dressing advice
Not recognising or asking about anticoagulation
errors_of_commission: Recommending an NSAID or aspirin for the pain in an anticoagulated patient
Prescribing antibiotics with no signs of infection
Starting topical field therapy (fluorouracil / imiquimod) via telehealth before the lesion is examined and SCC excluded
Escalating controllable minor bleeding to ED or 000 (over-triage)
Delivering a definitive benign diagnosis and discharging with no follow-up
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging in-person assessment of the changed lesion (to exclude SCC) AND appropriate haemostasis safety-netting. Reassure-and-discharge with no referral is a failing under-triage; over-escalation of controllable minor bleeding to ED/000, recommending an NSAID/aspirin, or empiric antibiotics are scored failures of commission.
